title: Questing Fiction - Finding the Holy Grail
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WKMTSCW51fI

Daniel (@dungeondive) follows up his Albion's Legacy review with a broader meditation on questing fiction itself. What is it about the quest that captivates us? From Greek mythology's Jason pursuing the Golden Fleece to modern collectors hunting their holy grails in thrift stores, the quest narrative speaks to something fundamental in how we experience adventure. Real collectors know the difference between buying something online and finding it "in the wild"—and that discovery *has* a story worth telling.

Daniel surveys the landscape of questing literature with infectious passion, spanning Robert E. Howard's magnificent Bran Mak Morn stories, Lord Dunsany's devastating brevity, Michael Moorcock's Elric pursuing the eternal city of Tanelorn, and of course Stephen King's masterwork Dark Tower series. He revisits Daryl Schweitzer's We Are All Legends—a genuinely haunting collection where a damned knight wanders in search of a way to die—and celebrates how questing fiction spans genres: pulp adventure (Raiders of the Lost Ark), westerns (Lonesome Dove), and epic fantasy alike. For Daniel and his viewers, the quest *is* the point, and good questing fiction reminds us why that hunger for discovery never fades.

----

Which questing narrative has most influenced how you approach games and collecting?
